name: Al Hall
abstract: Alfred Wesley Hall (March 18, 1915 – January 18, 1988) was an American jazz bassist.

works.t: The Love for Three Oranges, Op. 33t: March

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Dody Goodman in "Dody in Dixieland"

works.t: Call It Stormy Monday (But Tuesday Is Just as Bad)

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: My first opportunity to record under my own name... Al Hall was one of my favorite players... The addition of someone like Al Hall was a great comfort to me... I’m saying oh, wait a minute, you know. So it just made me more nervous and those two guys really settled me down.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: I was living with Al Hall, the bassist... I met him in Chicago, and he told me that any time I came to New York that I could stay with him until I found a place, you know. So I took him up on it.

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Al Hall was on bas$, and then I heard J.C. Heard in Detroit and brought him to New York, on drums -- J.C. was a swinging little drummer, he was a teenager when he joined my band

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: I was playing piano, with Lawrence Brown, Johnny Hodges, Al Hall the bass player, Denzil Best the drummer, and Billy Strayhorn was playing congas.

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Eldridge
about_quotes.evidence: You know that show, when I hired you guys, Teddy and Al Hall... Specs stayed on... Specs, Roy Eldridge, Al Hall, Teddy Wilson and Al Casey.

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Barefield
about_quotes.evidence: it was Dick Carey and Al Hall and Jimmy Crawford, Ray Deal and Max and myself.
